title: Ordinal Result Model
text:
This report adds the first result-probability model.
The ordered outcome is:
\[
Y_{i,m}^{ord} \in \{\text{loss}, \text{draw}, \text{win}\}
\]
with:
\[
\text{loss} < \text{draw} < \text{win}
\]
This is closer to betting-market comparison because it estimates match outcome probabilities.

title: Ordinal Result Model
text:
This report adds the first result-probability model.
The ordered outcome is:
\[
Y_{i,m}^{ord} \in \{\text{loss}, \text{draw}, \text{win}\}
\]
with:
\[
\text{loss} < \text{draw} < \text{win}
\]
This is closer to betting-market comparison because it estimates match outcome probabilities.

title: Ordinal Result Model
text:
2 Unit Of Observation
The data has one row per team-match.
If Argentina plays France, there are two rows:

Argentina result versus France.
France result versus Argentina.

title: Ordinal Result Model
text:
4 Model
The proportional-odds ordinal logistic model is:
\[
\text{logit}\left(P(Y_{i,m}^{ord} \leq k)\right)
= \theta_k - X_{i,m}\beta
\]
where:
\[
k \in \{\text{loss}, \text{draw}\}
\]
and:
\[
X_{i,m}\beta
= \beta_1 E_{i,m}^{pre}
+ \beta_2 E_{j,m}^{pre}
+ \beta_3 p_{i,m}
+ \beta_4 I(\text{listed home}_{i,m})
+ \beta_5 I(\text{neutral site}_m)
\]

title: Ordinal Result Model
text:
10 Interpretation
The model produces:
\[
P(\text{loss}), \quad P(\text{draw}), \quad P(\text{win})
\]
This is the shape needed for future odds comparison.
Still, this is a baseline:

The proportional-odds assumption may be too restrictive.
Paired team-match rows are not independent.
Draws usually need richer features.
Market odds will be the key benchmark once added.

title: Ordinal Result Model

source("R/11_fit_ordinal_result_model.R")

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
This site is the public-facing project notebook. It keeps the rendered analysis in HTML, hides code by default, and lets readers expand code with Show code.
No raw datasets, processed databases, API keys, .Renviron, credentials, or private files are published here.

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
1 Current Models


Show code
goals_metrics <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "goals_linear_model_metrics.csv"))
ordinal_metrics <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "result_ordinal_model_metrics.csv"))

if (nrow(goals_metrics) > 0 && nrow(ordinal_metrics) > 0) {
  data.frame(
    model = c("Goals OLS", "Ordinal result logistic"),
    target = c("team goals", "loss < draw < win"),
    rows_total = c(goals_metrics$rows_total[[1]], ordinal_metrics$rows_total[[1]]),
    primary_metric = c("test RMSE", "test accuracy"),
    value = c(
      round(goals_metrics$test_rmse[[1]], 4),
      round(ordinal_metrics$test_accuracy[[1]], 4)
    ),
    baseline_context = c(
      paste("mean test goals:", round(goals_metrics$mean_y_test[[1]], 4)),
      paste("majority baseline:", round(ordinal_metrics$baseline_majority_accuracy[[1]], 4))
    )
  )
} else {
  data.frame(note = "Model metrics are generated locally before rendering.")
}

2 Reports



2.1 Goals Model
OLS baseline for team goals scored from pre-match Elo, opponent Elo, expected result, home listing, and neutral-site status.
Open goals report



2.2 Result Model
Ordinal logistic baseline for loss < draw < win, producing win/draw/loss probability estimates.
Open result report

4 Refresh Options
Run these from RStudio.

4.1 Local Rebuild
Does not hit APIs or spend quota.


Show code
source("R/20_refresh_all.R")
refresh_world_cup_data(profile = "local-rebuild")




4.2 Free Source Refresh
Pulls free/no-key public sources and rebuilds the local site.


Show code
source("R/20_refresh_all.R")
refresh_world_cup_data(profile = "free-refresh")




4.3 Keyed APIs Later
Use after .env is configured locally.


Show code
source("R/20_refresh_all.R")
refresh_world_cup_data(
  profile = "free-refresh",
  include_keyed_apis = TRUE
)


Odds pulls require explicit quota permission.


Show code
source("R/20_refresh_all.R")
refresh_world_cup_data(
  profile = "free-refresh",
  include_keyed_apis = TRUE,
  include_odds_quota = TRUE
)

title: 2026 World Cup Betting Model
text:
5 Next Work

Add rolling recent-form features.
Add squad-strength and player-availability signals.
Add news-derived injury and lineup signals.
Add odds-implied probabilities.
Compare ordinal logistic, multinomial logistic, and paired-goal models.
Calibrate predicted probabilities.

title: Goals Linear Regression
text:
This report documents the first transparent baseline model for the World Cup prediction project.
The target is team goals scored in a historical international match:
\[
Y_{i,m} = \text{goals scored by team } i \text{ in match } m
\]
This is a baseline, not the final soccer model. Goals are non-negative counts, so future versions should compare Poisson, negative-binomial, bivariate Poisson, or Skellam-style models.

title: Goals Linear Regression
text:
This report documents the first transparent baseline model for the World Cup prediction project.
The target is team goals scored in a historical international match:
\[
Y_{i,m} = \text{goals scored by team } i \text{ in match } m
\]
This is a baseline, not the final soccer model. Goals are non-negative counts, so future versions should compare Poisson, negative-binomial, bivariate Poisson, or Skellam-style models.

title: Goals Linear Regression
text:
2 Unit Of Observation
The modeling frame has one row per team-match.
If Argentina plays France, the data has two rows:

Argentina’s goals as the target, with France as the opponent.
France’s goals as the target, with Argentina as the opponent.

title: Goals Linear Regression
text:

 8               4 1875-03-06 Scotland England  Friendly   FALSE       FALSE  
 9               5 1876-03-04 Scotland England  Friendly   TRUE        FALSE

# i 5 more variables: y_goals_for <dbl>, pre_elo <dbl>, opponent_pre_elo <dbl>,
#   elo_diff <dbl>, pre_match_expected_result <dbl>

title: Goals Linear Regression
text:
4 Elo Feature
For team \(i\) against opponent \(j\), expected result before kickoff is:
\[
p_{i,m} =
\frac{1}
{1 + 10^{-\left(E_{i,m}^{pre} + H_m - E_{j,m}^{pre}\right)/400}}
\]
Where:

\(E_{i,m}^{pre}\) is team \(i\)’s pre-match Elo.
\(E_{j,m}^{pre}\) is opponent pre-match Elo.
\(H_m\) is the home advantage adjustment.
\(p_{i,m}\) is expected result on a 0 to 1 scale.

title: Goals Linear Regression
Goals Linear Regression

metrics <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "goals_linear_model_metrics.csv"))
coefficients <- read_csv_if_exists(file.path(root, "data", "processed", "modeling", "goals_linear_model_coefficients.csv"))

# A tibble: 6 x 5
  term                      estimate std_error t_value   p_value
  <chr>                        <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>
1 (Intercept)                1.94    0.0552      35.1  2.25e-268
2 pre_elo                    0.00160 0.0000751   21.3  1.12e-100
3 opponent_pre_elo          -0.00228 0.0000751  -30.3  4.72e-201
4 pre_match_expected_result  0.773   0.0707      10.9  8.33e- 28
5 listed_homeTRUE            0.395   0.0154      25.7  9.21e-145
6 neutralTRUE                0.0623  0.0118       5.28 1.33e-  7

title: Goals Linear Regression
text:
7 Interpretation
The signs match soccer intuition:

pre_elo is positive: stronger teams score more.
opponent_pre_elo is negative: stronger opponents suppress scoring.
pre_match_expected_result is positive: teams expected to perform better score more.
listed_homeTRUE is positive: listed home teams score more.

title: Goals Linear Regression
